summary: Caffeine is well established as an acute alertness and concentration aid; consumers also strongly associate coffee with energy.
evidence_source: Harvard Nutrition Source; Reichert 2022 review on adenosine/caffeine
perception_source: Drive Research 2024 coffee survey: energy is a major reason for drinking coffee

summary: Caffeine can support concentration short-term, but consumers often frame coffee more broadly as a productivity tool.
perception_source: Drive Research 2024 coffee survey: productivity is reported as a reason for drinking coffee

summary: Coffee is associated with positive emotions such as energy, satisfaction and pleasure, while health evidence is less direct than for alertness.
perception_source: Samoggia & Riedel 2020 on coffee emotions and perceptions

summary: The social benefit is mainly cultural and experiential rather than a direct biomedical effect.
Meta-analyses associate coffee consumption with lower type 2 diabetes risk, but this is not an everyday perceived benefit for many consumers.
perception_source: Drive Research 2024: health benefits are a less common stated reason than taste/energy

summary: Umbrella-review evidence suggests coffee is more often associated with benefit than harm, although causality remains uncertain.
evidence_source: Poole et al. 2017 BMJ umbrella review

summary: Appetite suppression is reported by some consumers, but meaningful weight-loss benefit is not among coffee's strongest evidence-backed effects.
evidence_source: Poole et al. 2017 BMJ umbrella review; Harvard Nutrition Source
perception_source: Drive Research 2024 survey: appetite suppression reported as a reason

summary: Some observational evidence links coffee with lower mortality risk, but this is less visible in everyday consumer motivations.
evidence_source: Poole et al. 2017 BMJ umbrella review
perception_source: Drive Research 2024: health benefits are less common than taste and energy motivations
